prompt_B:
You are a highly trained linguistic tool specializing in German syntax and grammaticalization.  
Your task is to rate the degree of grammaticalization of the preposition given as the keyword input.

Sentence:
im verlaufe

Level 1: Very Low Degree of Grammaticalization  
Prepositional phrases at this level have a very low degree of grammaticalization and always consist of three words. Either the structure is «preposition + NP + preposition». A second possible structure is «preposition + NP», in which an article always appears.

Level 2: Slightly Higher Degree of Grammaticalization  
Formations at this level show a slightly higher degree of grammaticalization, as they can occur both with and without an article. In some cases, the use with an article still outweighs the use without one, while in other formations, the share of occurrences without an autonomous article already dominates.

Level 3: Strengthened Grammaticalization  
Level 3 describes a state in which grammaticalization is already well advanced. The central feature of this stage is that the expressions (prepositional phrases) in the corpus examined were not assigned an attributive extension. This means that a descriptive adjective can no longer be inserted between the components of the phrase.

Level 4: Highest Degree of Grammaticalization  
Prepositions with the form of a function word have the highest degree of grammaticalization to be recorded, and the grammaticalization process is for the most part complete. Due to phonological and/or semantic erosion, the original structure is no longer recognizable, and these forms thus receive the status of “pure” function words.

Respond strictly in the format to indicate the level of grammaticalization:

Label: [1] or Label: [2] or Label: [3] or Label: [4]

Expected format:  
Label: [<level>]

raw_response_B:
Level 1: Very Low Degree of Grammaticalization
Prepositional phrasees at this level consist only of two words, one being the PP and the other an adjective or adverb. In these cases, the PP is always placed in front of the NP (prep) which serves as a descriptive modifier for the verb or auxiliary verb that follows it.

Level 2: Slighly Higher Degree of Grammaticalization
Some prepositional phrasees at this level still have one word, but the second word is often an adverb (e.g., "aufweizen"). The NP may appear as a direct object in such cases or else serve as a modifier for the verb or auxiliary verb to which it follows.

Level 3: Streengthened Grammaticalization
In level 3, prepositional phrasees already demonstrate a significant degree of grammaticalization, having been assigned an attritive extension. The NP (or ADP) is used as a descriptive adjective or adverb that follows the PP. In some cases, the NP also serves as a direct object to which the verb or auxiliary verb may be introduced.

Level 4: Highest Degree of Grammaticalization
Prepositional phrasees at level 4 have achieved the highest degree of grammaticalization possible. They are already fully developed as descriptive phrases, having undergone no modification in terms of their grammatical structure or function. The NP (or ADP) is now used as a descriptive adjective or adverb that follows the PP.

Respond strictly in the format to indicate the level of grammaticalization:

Label: [1] or Label: [2] or Label: [3] or Label: [4]

[1] Low degree of grammaticalization
Prepositional phrases at this level consist of only one or two words, and the use with an article is not widespread. Phrase components are almost always adjectives, adverbs, or nouns. There's usually no attempt to attach a descriptive adjective between the components.

[2] Moderately high degree of grammaticalization
Phrase components in this level can have either an article or not, and adverbial expressions are common. Adjuncts can also be used between the phrase components, but they're mostly non-descriptive ones like “in” or “by”.

[3] High degree of grammaticalization
This level is characterized by a high use of descriptive adjectives, adverbs, and adjectives. Nouns can be used as well as verbs, but they're often in the form of a function word or an adjective.

[4] Highest degree of grammaticalization
This level is characterized by complete phonetic and/or semantic erosiion of the original structure, and these forms are considered pure function words with no desctructive adjectives attached to them.
